description: Conditional tenant scoping allows cross-tenant data access when tenantId is null
intended_behavior: GET /api/v1/accounts should always scope results to the authenticated user's tenant. No user should see another tenant's accounts.
actual_behavior: Line 34: `if (ctx.tenantId) where.tenantId = ctx.tenantId;` — if ctx.tenantId is null (which is valid per the ApiContext type where tenantId is `string | null`), the filter is never applied and ALL tenants' email accounts are returned. This same pattern appears in multiple files.
proposed_fix: Always include tenantId in the where clause. If tenantId is null, return an empty result or an error: `where.tenantId = ctx.tenantId ?? '__NONE__';` or add a guard: `if (!ctx.tenantId) return error('FORBIDDEN', 'Tenant not assigned', 403);`

description: Duplicate email check in POST accounts uses findUnique on email without tenant scoping
intended_behavior: The duplicate check should verify uniqueness within the tenant's accounts, not globally. Different tenants should be able to register the same email.
actual_behavior: Line 113: `ctx.db.emailAccount.findUnique({ where: { email } })` — this checks the global unique constraint, not tenant-scoped. Since the schema has `email String @unique`, this is a DB-level constraint, so it's correct for preventing collisions, but it also means Tenant A creating 'user@example.com' prevents Tenant B from doing the same. This is a design-level issue in the Prisma schema (email is globally unique, not per-tenant unique).
proposed_fix: If multi-tenant email overlap is intended, change the Prisma schema to use `@@unique([email, tenantId])` instead of `@unique` on email. If global uniqueness is intentional, this is acceptable but should be documented.

description: Conditional tenant scoping on social accounts GET allows cross-tenant data leak
intended_behavior: Listing social accounts should be scoped to the authenticated user's tenant.
actual_behavior: Line 34: `where: { id, ...(ctx.tenantId ? { tenantId: ctx.tenantId } : {}) }` — when tenantId is null, the spread results in an empty object and the query has no tenant filter, returning the email account regardless of which tenant it belongs to.
proposed_fix: Remove the conditional: always filter by tenantId. If tenantId is null, return 403 or notFound.

description: Conditional tenant scoping on social accounts POST allows creating resources under other tenants' email accounts
intended_behavior: Creating a social account should only be allowed under an email account owned by the authenticated user's tenant.
actual_behavior: Line 91: `where: { id, ...(ctx.tenantId ? { tenantId: ctx.tenantId } : {}) }` — same pattern as P2C_003. With a null tenantId, an attacker could create social accounts under any email account.
proposed_fix: Always scope by tenantId. If tenantId is null, return 403.

description: Conditional tenant scoping on channels GET allows cross-tenant channel enumeration
intended_behavior: Channel listing should always be scoped to the authenticated user's tenant.
actual_behavior: Line 38: `if (ctx.tenantId) { ... }` — when tenantId is null, no tenant filter is applied and channels from all tenants are returned.
proposed_fix: Always apply the socialAccount -> emailAccount -> tenantId chain filter. Guard against null tenantId.

description: Conditional tenant scoping on channel POST allows linking channels to other tenants' social accounts
intended_behavior: Channel creation should only be allowed under social accounts owned by the authenticated user's tenant.
actual_behavior: Line 146: `...(ctx.tenantId ? { emailAccount: { tenantId: ctx.tenantId } } : {})` — with null tenantId, any social account can be used.
proposed_fix: Always include the emailAccount.tenantId filter.

description: Channel DELETE uses findUnique without tenant scoping, then checks tenant manually — TOCTOU race
intended_behavior: Channel DELETE should atomically verify ownership and delete.
actual_behavior: Lines 169-186: Uses `findUnique({ where: { id } })` (no tenant filter), then checks `channel.socialAccount.emailAccount.tenantId !== ctx.tenantId` separately. Additionally, line 184: `if (ctx.tenantId && ...)` — if ctx.tenantId is null, the ownership check is skipped entirely, allowing deletion of any channel.
proposed_fix: Use `findFirst` with the full tenant chain filter (matching the GET and PUT patterns) instead of findUnique + manual check. Remove the `ctx.tenantId &&` guard.

description: Conditional tenant scoping on affiliate pool DELETE allows cross-tenant pool manipulation
intended_behavior: Affiliate pool operations should be scoped to the authenticated user's tenant.
actual_behavior: Line 35: `...(ctx.tenantId ? { socialAccount: { emailAccount: { tenantId: ctx.tenantId } } } : {})` — null tenantId bypasses scoping. Same pattern in GET (line 74) and POST (line 126).
proposed_fix: Always include the tenant chain filter. Guard against null tenantId.

description: Conditional tenant scoping on schedule GET allows viewing all tenants' scheduled posts
intended_behavior: Scheduled post listing should be scoped to the authenticated user's tenant.
actual_behavior: Line 27: `if (ctx.tenantId) { ... }` — null tenantId means no tenant filter, exposing all scheduled posts from all tenants.
proposed_fix: Always apply the tenant filter chain. Guard against null tenantId.

description: Conditional tenant scoping on schedule POST content verification allows cross-tenant scheduling
intended_behavior: Content and channel ownership should be verified against the authenticated user's tenant before scheduling.
actual_behavior: Lines 104-117: `const tenantFilter = ctx.tenantId ? { ... } : {};` and `...(ctx.tenantId ? { channel: tenantFilter } : {})` — with null tenantId, any content from any tenant can be scheduled to any channel.
proposed_fix: Always apply tenant scoping on content and channel lookups.

description: Conditional tenant scoping on schedule PUT allows rescheduling other tenants' posts
intended_behavior: Only the owning tenant should be able to reschedule a scheduled post.
actual_behavior: Lines 38-46: `...(ctx.tenantId ? { channel: { ... } } : {})` — null tenantId bypasses the ownership chain. Same pattern in DELETE (lines 120-129).
proposed_fix: Always include the tenant chain filter.

description: Sync job enqueued without tenantId in job data, worker cannot verify tenant ownership
intended_behavior: Job data should carry tenantId so the worker can scope its DB operations.
actual_behavior: Line 37: `addJob('account', 'account:sync', { socialAccountId: socialId })` — only socialAccountId is passed. The worker (handleSync) does not have tenantId and operates on the global DB. While the API route verifies ownership before enqueuing, the worker itself has no tenant context for any downstream operations.
proposed_fix: Include tenantId in job data: `{ socialAccountId: socialId, tenantId: ctx.tenantId }`

description: Health check job enqueued without tenantId in job data
intended_behavior: Job data should include tenantId for consistent scoping in workers.
actual_behavior: Same as P2C_012 — only socialAccountId is passed, no tenantId.
proposed_fix: Include tenantId in job data.

description: Warm route swallows invalid JSON body and Zod parse errors silently
intended_behavior: If the body exists but is malformed or fails Zod validation, the user should be informed.
actual_behavior: Lines 30-35: The inner try/catch catches both JSON parse errors AND Zod validation errors (e.g., durationMinutes: 'abc' or durationMinutes: 999) and silently falls through with defaults. A user sending `{"durationMinutes": 500}` would not get a validation error — it would just use defaults.
proposed_fix: Use safeParse instead of parse in a try/catch. If the body is present but invalid, return a validationError. If the body is missing/empty, use defaults.

description: CSV parsing is naive — does not handle quoted fields with commas, newlines in quoted strings, or CSV injection
intended_behavior: CSV parsing should correctly handle standard CSV format including quoted fields, embedded commas, and embedded newlines.
actual_behavior: Line 47: `line.split(',')` — this simple split does not handle commas within quoted fields (e.g., `"user,name@email.com"` would be split incorrectly). Also, email/password fields from CSV are only validated by the `accounts.length === 0` check and basic email/password presence, not by the AccountEntrySchema Zod validator that's used for JSON input.
proposed_fix: Use a proper CSV parser (e.g., papaparse or csv-parse), or validate CSV entries against AccountEntrySchema. Also validate that tier values from CSV match valid tiers before the general loop.

description: CSV entries skip Zod schema validation that JSON entries go through
intended_behavior: Both JSON and CSV entries should be validated by the same schema.
actual_behavior: JSON entries are validated via BulkImportSchema (which delegates to AccountEntrySchema with email(), min(1), max(256), etc.), but CSV entries are only checked for presence of email and password (lines 78, 84), without email format validation or password length limits.
proposed_fix: Apply AccountEntrySchema validation to each CSV-parsed entry.

description: UpdateAccountSchema status enum does not match Prisma schema valid statuses
intended_behavior: The Zod validation should permit exactly the statuses the Prisma schema expects.
actual_behavior: Line 9: `z.enum(['active', 'disabled', 'flagged', 'pending'])` — the Prisma schema comment on EmailAccount.status says 'active, disabled, flagged, pending' which matches. However, the GET route's filter (accounts/route.ts line 27) allows filtering by 'warming', 'cooldown', 'suspended', 'banned' — these statuses cannot be set via the PUT route. If accounts can reach these statuses via other paths (worker), the PUT route cannot transition them back without direct DB manipulation. This may be intentional, but is worth noting.
proposed_fix: Either expand the UpdateAccountSchema enum to include all valid statuses, or document that certain statuses are worker-managed only.

description: healthScore used as number in arithmetic but the field is Int — `account.healthScore + 1` could work but the type context is ambiguous due to Prisma returning number for Int
intended_behavior: Health score arithmetic should produce valid integer values within bounds.
actual_behavior: Lines 315-317: `Math.min(100, account.healthScore + 1)` and `Math.max(0, account.healthScore - 10)` — this is actually fine since Prisma Int returns as JavaScript number. However, the score is clamped to [0, 100] but the threshold check on line 333 uses `account.healthScore >= 30` (old value) to decide if an alert should be created. This correctly detects a crossing from >=30 to <30. No actual bug here.
proposed_fix: No fix needed — the logic is correct. Marking as reviewed.

description: handleSeasoningEnroll creates enrollment without checking if one already exists for the same cohort/email/platform combo
intended_behavior: Enrollment creation should be idempotent or check for existing enrollment to avoid duplicates.
actual_behavior: Line 485: `db.seasoningEnrollment.create(...)` — the Prisma schema has `@@unique([cohortId, emailAccountId, platform])` so a duplicate would throw a Prisma unique constraint error, which would propagate as an unhandled exception. The function does not catch this case specifically.
proposed_fix: Either use `upsert` or catch the unique constraint error and return a meaningful result indicating the enrollment already exists.

description: Cohort totalAccounts incremented outside a transaction — race condition with concurrent enrollments
intended_behavior: Enrollment creation and cohort count update should be atomic.
actual_behavior: Lines 485-498: `db.seasoningEnrollment.create(...)` and `db.seasoningCohort.update({ data: { totalAccounts: { increment: 1 } } })` are two separate queries. If two enroll jobs run concurrently, both could read the same count and increment to the same value, losing one count. The `{ increment: 1 }` Prisma operator is atomic at the DB level, so the count itself won't be lost, but the enrollment creation + count update are not transactional — if the enrollment fails after the count update, the count will be inflated.
proposed_fix: Wrap both operations in a `db.$transaction([...])` to ensure atomicity.

description: handleSeasoningCheckDue queries all enrollments across all tenants without tenant scoping
intended_behavior: The check-due scanner should process enrollments, but since it's a system job (no tenant context), this may be intentional.
actual_behavior: Lines 843-848: `db.seasoningEnrollment.findMany({ where: { nextScheduledAt: { lte: now }, status: { in: [...] } } })` — no tenant filter. As a system-level background job, this is likely intentional (it needs to process all tenants). However, the queued warm jobs include tenantId from `enrollment.cohort.tenantId`, which is good.
proposed_fix: No fix needed if this is designed as a global system job. Add a comment clarifying this is intentionally cross-tenant for the system scanner.

description: handleSeasoningGraduate updates social account status without verifying it exists
intended_behavior: Before updating a social account, verify it exists to avoid a Prisma error.
actual_behavior: Line 920: `if (data.socialAccountId) { await db.socialAccount.update(...) }` — this checks if the ID is provided in job data, but does not verify the social account still exists in the DB. If the social account was deleted between job enqueue and processing, Prisma will throw a P2025 'Record not found' error.
proposed_fix: Use `updateMany` which won't throw on zero matches, or add a findUnique check first.

description: checkScheduledPosts queries all due posts without a limit, could overwhelm the worker
intended_behavior: The scheduled post checker should process due posts in manageable batches.
actual_behavior: Lines 193-202: `db.scheduledPost.findMany({ where: { scheduledAt: { lte: new Date() }, status: 'scheduled' } })` — no `take` limit. If the system has been down and thousands of posts are due, this query could return a massive result set and the subsequent for-loop would enqueue thousands of jobs at once.
proposed_fix: Add `take: 50` (or similar batch limit) and process in batches. The repeatable job runs every 60s, so batching naturally handles backlog.

description: handleSchedule assumes content.channel.socialAccount exists without null check
intended_behavior: The handler should verify the content has a valid channel with a social account before using it.
actual_behavior: Line 250: `socialAccountId: content.channel.socialAccount.id` — if the channel's social account was deleted between content creation and scheduling, this would throw a TypeError. Additionally, there is no validation that `data.channelId` matches `content.channelId` — a content item could be scheduled to a different channel than it belongs to.
proposed_fix: Add null checks for content.channel and content.channel.socialAccount. Validate that data.channelId matches content.channelId.

description: checkScheduledPosts has a TOCTOU race — status could change between findMany and update
intended_behavior: A scheduled post should only be enqueued once.
actual_behavior: Lines 193-223: The function queries posts with `status: 'scheduled'`, then enqueues a job, then updates status to 'queued'. If two instances of checkScheduledPosts run concurrently (e.g., restart with overlapping repeatable jobs), the same post could be found in both runs before either updates the status, leading to duplicate publish attempts.
proposed_fix: Use an atomic update with a WHERE clause that includes the status check: `updateMany({ where: { id: post.id, status: 'scheduled' }, data: { status: 'queued' } })` and only enqueue the job if the update affected 1 row.

description: Failed post retryCount is set to attemptsMade which may not reflect actual retries
intended_behavior: The retryCount should accurately reflect the number of attempts made.
actual_behavior: Line 162: `retryCount: job.attemptsMade` — BullMQ's `attemptsMade` starts at 1 for the first attempt, so after the first failure `retryCount` will be 1, after second it will be 2, etc. However, the final-attempt alert check (line 167) uses `job.attemptsMade >= maxAttempts`, which is correct since BullMQ increments attemptsMade before calling the processor on retry. This logic is actually correct but potentially confusing.
proposed_fix: No fix needed — logic is correct. Add a comment for clarity.

description: determineNextAction returns 'warm' with advanceTo param when phase should advance, but the warm handler may not process the advance correctly
intended_behavior: When shouldAdvancePhase returns true, the enrollment should be advanced to the next phase.
actual_behavior: Line 63: `return { type: 'warm', enrollmentId: id, params: { advanceTo: next } }` — the warm action is returned with an `advanceTo` parameter, but the worker's handleSeasoningWarm function (account.worker.ts) does not check for or process an `advanceTo` field in the job data. The worker's phase advancement logic (lines 784-799) relies on time-based checks instead. The orchestrator's state machine and the worker's actual behavior are disconnected.
proposed_fix: Either: (a) have the worker respect the `advanceTo` param from the orchestrator, or (b) remove the `advanceTo` param from the orchestrator since the worker handles advancement independently. Currently the orchestrator logic is unused.

description: shouldGraduate does not check minHealthScore from graduation criteria
intended_behavior: Graduation should verify the account meets the minimum health score requirement.
actual_behavior: The GraduationCriteria interface includes `minHealthScore: number` (set to 70 in DEFAULT_SEASONING_SCHEDULE), but the `shouldGraduate` function (lines 127-145) never checks the enrollment's health score against this criterion. The enrollment record does not carry healthScore, and the social account's healthScore is not accessed.
proposed_fix: Either pass the social account's healthScore to shouldGraduate and check against criteria.minHealthScore, or remove minHealthScore from GraduationCriteria if it's not meant to be checked at this level.

description: Seasoning warm handler assigns enrollment status directly from phase names, conflating status and phase concepts
intended_behavior: Enrollment status should represent the enrollment lifecycle state, while currentPhase represents the seasoning phase. They may differ (e.g., status=paused while currentPhase=phase_3).
actual_behavior: Lines 790-796: `newStatus = nextPhase; newPhase = nextPhase;` — the status field is set to the phase name (e.g., 'phase_2', 'phase_3'). While the EnrollmentStatus type includes phase names as valid values, this tight coupling means the status field and currentPhase field are always identical during normal progression, making one of them redundant. More importantly, this makes the pause/resume flow unclear — after resuming from 'paused', what should status be?
proposed_fix: Consider separating status from phase: status could be 'active' while currentPhase tracks progression. This is more of a design observation than a bug.

description: failureCount is reset to 0 on any successful warm session, not tracking consecutive failures correctly
intended_behavior: failureCount should track consecutive failures for risk assessment.
actual_behavior: Line 821: `failureCount: success ? 0 : { increment: 1 }` — on any success, failureCount resets to 0. This means a pattern of success-fail-success-fail would never trigger the `maxConsecutiveFailures` threshold (3) in assessRisk. This is actually correct behavior for 'consecutive' failures but could mask patterns of intermittent failures.
proposed_fix: No fix needed for consecutive failure tracking. Consider adding a separate `totalFailureCount` for overall failure rate monitoring.

description: channelId filter on scheduled posts GET is not validated as UUID and not tenant-scoped
intended_behavior: The channelId filter should validate format and verify the channel belongs to the user's tenant.
actual_behavior: Line 37: `if (channelId) where.channelId = channelId;` — channelId is taken directly from query params without UUID validation. While Prisma would fail on an invalid UUID format, it's inconsistent with other endpoints that validate UUIDs. More importantly, a user could filter by a channelId belonging to a different tenant (though the tenant filter on the query would prevent data leakage IF tenantId is non-null — see P2C_009).
proposed_fix: Validate channelId as UUID using isUUID().

description: Scheduled post status transition from 'queued' to 'posting' is not checked — post may be cancelled between enqueue and process
intended_behavior: Before publishing, the worker should verify the post is still in a publishable state (not cancelled).
actual_behavior: Line 42: `await db.scheduledPost.update({ where: { id: scheduledPost.id }, data: { status: 'posting' } })` — the worker unconditionally sets status to 'posting' without checking the current status. If a user cancels the post (sets status to 'cancelled') between when it was enqueued by checkScheduledPosts and when the worker picks it up, the worker will override the cancellation and proceed with posting.
proposed_fix: Add a status check: `if (scheduledPost.status === 'cancelled') return;` before proceeding. Or use updateMany with a where clause including acceptable statuses.

description: Missing ENCRYPTION_KEY throws a generic error that gets reported as a posting failure
intended_behavior: Configuration errors should be distinguishable from posting failures.
actual_behavior: Line 50: `if (!socialAccount.credentialsEnc || !config.ENCRYPTION_KEY)` throws a generic Error, which is caught by the catch block and treated as a posting failure, potentially creating misleading alerts about 'failed to post to platform'.
proposed_fix: Throw a distinct error type or handle this case separately to create a more descriptive alert.

description: Platform and status query params on social accounts GET are not validated against allowed values
intended_behavior: Filter params should be validated to prevent passing invalid values to Prisma.
actual_behavior: Lines 28-29: `platform` and `status` are passed directly to the Prisma where clause without validation. Unlike the accounts GET route (which validates status/tier against allowed lists), the socials GET passes any arbitrary string as platform or status filter. Prisma will still work (returning empty results for non-matching values), but it's inconsistent.
proposed_fix: Add validation: `const validPlatforms = ['youtube', 'tiktok', 'instagram', 'facebook'];` and only apply filter if value is valid.

description: Seasoning worker reuses processAccountJob with 'as any' cast
intended_behavior: The seasoning worker should have a properly typed job processor.
actual_behavior: Line 969: `createWorker('seasoning', processAccountJob as any, ...)` — the processAccountJob function handles both account and seasoning jobs, but its type signature expects `Job` (from the account queue types). The `as any` cast suppresses type checking.
proposed_fix: Create a separate processSeasoning function or use a union type for the job processor.

description: Affiliate product lookup in POST is not tenant-scoped — any product from any tenant can be added to a channel's pool
intended_behavior: Only affiliate products belonging to (or visible to) the authenticated user's tenant should be addable to a channel's pool.
actual_behavior: Line 140: `ctx.db.affiliateProduct.findUnique({ where: { id: affiliateProductId } })` — no tenant filter. If AffiliateProduct has a tenantId or is scoped somehow, this lookup does not enforce it.
proposed_fix: Check if AffiliateProduct has tenant scoping in the schema and add the appropriate filter.

description: scheduledAt future check uses local server time comparison
intended_behavior: The 'must be in the future' check should be robust against timezone differences between client and server.
actual_behavior: Line 99: `if (scheduledDate <= new Date())` — compares the parsed ISO date against server's current time. If the client sends a time that is in the future in their timezone but in the past in the server's timezone, it would be rejected. This is generally fine for ISO dates with timezone offsets, but could be surprising if the client sends a naive date string.
proposed_fix: The Zod schema validates the string is parseable. Consider documenting that all dates should be in UTC or include timezone offsets.

description: handleSeasoningWarm does not throw on failure, unlike handleSeasoningSignup
intended_behavior: Error handling should be consistent across handlers — either throw to trigger BullMQ retries or handle gracefully.
actual_behavior: handleSeasoningSignup (line 641) throws on error (triggering BullMQ retry), but handleSeasoningWarm silently logs errors and updates the enrollment with failure info. This means warm failures won't be retried by BullMQ even though the job is configured with `attempts: 2`.
proposed_fix: If retries are desired for warm failures, throw the error after updating the enrollment. If not, remove the `attempts: 2` configuration from the warm job enqueue.
